html:
[426]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 不知何许人 - Phrase meaning 'unknown origin' or 'of uncertain background.'
  • 自称年已数百岁 - Phrase meaning 'claimed to be several hundred years old,' suggesting an exaggerated or mythical claim of age.
  • 无可考也 - Means 'cannot be verified' or 'there is no record,' indicating that the person's claim is unverifiable.
  • 支离荒杳 - Means 'fragmented and remote,' describing the person's statements as disjointed and difficult to understand.
  • 前明醒神之流 - Refers to 'those of the previous dynasty who were enlightened or awakened,' possibly alluding to mystics or spiritual figures from the Ming dynasty.
  • 符药治病 - Means 'using talismans and medicine to treat illnesses,' referring to traditional Chinese medical practices.
  • 时有小验 - Means 'sometimes there are minor successes,' indicating that the treatments occasionally work.
  • 符箓烧炼之术 - Refers to 'the art of talismanic incantations and alchemical practices,' describing the methods used by the person.
  • 有时而效，有时而不效 - Means 'sometimes effective, sometimes not,' indicating that the methods are inconsistent in their results.
  • 符箓烧炼之术有时而效，有时而不效也 - Phrase meaning 'the art of talismanic incantations and alchemical practices is sometimes effective, sometimes not,' emphasizing the inconsistency of the methods.
  • 卖药道士 - Refers to 'medicine-selling Taoist priests,' describing people who sell potions and perform rituals.
  • 说法禅僧 - Refers to 'preaching Zen monks,' describing people who teach Buddhist doctrines.
  • 神仙必有 - Means 'there must be immortals,' expressing belief in the existence of immortals.
  • 然非今之卖药道士 - Means 'but not the current medicine-selling Taoist priests,' distinguishing true immortals from fraudulent practitioners.
  • 佛菩萨必有 - Means 'there must be Buddhas and Bodhisattvas,' expressing belief in the existence of enlightened beings.
  • 然非今之说法禅僧 - Means 'but not the current preaching Zen monks,' distinguishing true enlightened beings from fraudulent practitioners.
  • 斯真千古持平之论矣 - Means 'this is truly a fair and balanced view for all time,' expressing approval of the statement.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 李老人 - The name of an elderly man, whose identity is unknown.
  • 先师钱文敏公 - Refers to a respected teacher, Mr. Qian Wenmin, who was a scholar and official.
  • 陈裕斋倪余疆 - Refers to two individuals, Chen Yuzhai and Ni Yujiang, who were likely scholars or officials.
  • 水月庵 - Refers to a temple or monastery named 'Shuiyue An,' where the second son of Mr. Qian Wenmin lived.
  • 符药治病 - Refers to traditional Chinese medical practices involving talismans and medicine.
  • 割指上疣赘 - Refers to a specific medical procedure, possibly involving cutting off a wart or growth.
  • 创发病卒 - Refers to the death of Mr. Qian Wenmin due to complications from the medical procedure.
  • 符箓烧炼之术 - Refers to traditional Chinese practices involving talismanic incantations and alchemical practices.
  • 卖药道士 - Refers to medicine-selling Taoist priests, who were often associated with fraudulent practices.
  • 说法禅僧 - Refers to preaching Zen monks, who were often associated with fraudulent teachings.
  • 神仙 - Refers to immortals or enlightened beings in Chinese mythology.
  • 佛菩萨 - Refers to Buddhas and Bodhisattvas in Buddhism.
  • 刘文正公 - Refers to a respected teacher, Mr. Liu Wenzheng, who was a scholar and official.
  • 斯真千古持平之论矣 - Refers to a statement expressing a fair and balanced view for all time.

Proper Nouns

  • 李老人 - The name of an elderly man.
  • 钱文敏公 - The name of a respected teacher, Mr. Qian Wenmin.
  • 陈裕斋 - The name of an individual, Chen Yuzhai.
  • 倪余疆 - The name of an individual, Ni Yujiang.
  • 水月庵 - The name of a temple or monastery.
  • 刘文正公 - The name of a respected teacher, Mr. Liu Wenzheng.

[427]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 典试 - Refers to the examination of candidates for the imperial examinations.
  • 相法及推算八字五星 - Refers to physiognomy and the calculation of the eight characters (八字) and the five planets (五星).
  • 刑部 - Refers to the Ministry of Justice in the Qing dynasty.
  • 堂参 - Refers to the daily assembly of officials in the government office.
  • 乞假 - Refers to requesting leave or vacation.
  • 子平家 - Refers to the school of thought that believes fate is predetermined.
  • 堪舆家 - Refers to the school of thought that believes fate can be changed through geomancy.
  • 命有定 - Refers to the belief that fate is predetermined.
  • 命可移 - Refers to the belief that fate can be changed.
  • 得其通 - Refers to understanding the truth or essence of something.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 甲辰 - Refers to the year 1804 in the Jiaqing era of the Qing dynasty.
  • 刑部侍郎 - Refers to the Deputy Minister of Justice in the Qing dynasty.
  • 阮吾山 - Refers to a high-ranking official named Ruan Wushan.
  • 杜凝台 - Refers to a high-ranking official named Du Ningtai.
  • 伊犁 - Refers to a region in present-day Xinjiang, China.
  • 子平家 - Refers to the school of thought that believes fate is predetermined, named after the famous astrologer and physiognomist Zhu Xi.
  • 堪舆家 - Refers to the school of thought that believes fate can be changed through geomancy, also known as feng shui.

Proper Nouns

  • 杨主事盻 - Refers to a minor official named Yang Pian.
  • 阮吾山 - Refers to a high-ranking official named Ruan Wushan.
  • 杜凝台 - Refers to a high-ranking official named Du Ningtai.
  • 伊犁 - Refers to a region in present-day Xinjiang, China.
  • 子平家 - Refers to the school of thought that believes fate is predetermined, named after the famous astrologer and physiognomist Zhu Xi.
  • 堪舆家 - Refers to the school of thought that believes fate can be changed through geomancy, also known as feng shui.

[428]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 遣犯 - Refers to a person who has been exiled or banished; should be rendered to convey the idea of exile or punishment.
  • 病瘵 - Means 'suffering from consumption' or 'tuberculosis'; '瘵' specifically refers to tuberculosis, a serious and often fatal disease in ancient times.
  • 垂尽 - Literally means 'on the verge of death'; indicates that the woman is near death.
  • 官田耕作 - Refers to farming government-owned land, which was a common form of labor for exiles or convicts.
  • 舁归 - Means 'to carry back' or 'to take back'; refers to the act of transporting someone.
  • 伉俪之盟 - Literally means 'alliance of husband and wife'; here it is used to express a promise of marriage.
  • 不畏嫌疑 - Means 'without fear of suspicion' or 'without worrying about gossip'; suggests that the person acts without concern for what others might think.
  • 搔仰抚摩 - Refers to physical gestures of care, such as scratching, lifting, and stroking, which are expressions of affection and tenderness.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 彭杞 - The name of the exiled convict, who is the subject of the story.
  • 杨皃 - The name of the person who takes pity on the woman and agrees to care for her.
  • 病瘵 - Tuberculosis was a widespread and deadly disease in ancient China, often associated with poverty and poor living conditions.
  • 官田 - Government-owned land was often assigned to exiles or convicts as a form of punishment, requiring them to work to support themselves.
  • 遣犯 - Exile or banishment was a common form of punishment in ancient China, often involving relocation to remote areas where the exiles were forced to live and work under harsh conditions.

Proper Nouns

  • 彭杞 - The name of the exiled convict.
  • 杨皃 - The name of the person who takes pity on the woman and cares for her.

[429]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 然病骸憔悴 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'though his body was emaciated and worn out,' indicating physical decline.
  • 迄未能一荐枕衾 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'he had never been able to share a bed with her,' referring to sexual intimacy.
  • 实多愧负 - Means 'he felt deeply ashamed and guilty,' expressing emotional burden.
  • 若殁而无鬼 - Conditional phrase meaning 'if there is no soul after death,' introducing a hypothetical scenario.
  • 夫复何言 - Rhetorical expression meaning 'what more can be said,' indicating resignation.
  • 若魂魄有知 - Another conditional phrase meaning 'if the soul has consciousness,' suggesting a belief in an afterlife.
  • 当必有以奉报 - Means 'he would surely find a way to repay,' expressing a sense of duty or promise.
  • 呜咽而终 - Vivid description meaning 'he died weeping,' conveying a poignant end.
  • 杨涕泣葬之 - Means 'Yang buried him with tears,' indicating emotional grief.
  • 夜夜梦女来狎昵欢好 - Vivid description meaning 'every night he dreamed of her coming close and being intimate,' evoking recurring dreams.
  • 一若生人 - Means 'as if she were alive,' suggesting a lifelike presence in dreams.
  • 醒则无所睹 - Means 'when he woke up, he saw nothing,' contrasting the dream with reality.
  • 夜中呼之终不出 - Means 'calling her out in the night, she never appeared,' indicating a futile attempt to summon her.
  • 才一交睫 - Means 'as soon as he closed his eyes,' referring to the moment of falling asleep.
  • 即弛服横陈矣 - Means 'she would immediately appear naked and lying down,' describing the dream's vividness.
  • 往来既久 - Means 'after repeated occurrences,' indicating a pattern of recurring dreams.
  • 梦中亦知是梦 - Means 'he knew it was a dream while dreaming,' suggesting a meta-awareness within the dream.
  • 诘以不肯现形之由 - Means 'he questioned her about why she wouldn't appear in her true form,' expressing curiosity.
  • 吾闻诸鬼云 - Means 'I have heard from the spirits,' indicating a source of information.
  • 人阳而鬼阴 - Philosophical statement meaning 'humans are yang while ghosts are yin,' referring to the yin-yang duality.
  • 以阴侵阳 - Means 'using yin to invade yang,' suggesting a conflict between the spiritual and the physical.
  • 必为人害 - Means 'it would surely harm humans,' indicating a potential danger.
  • 惟睡则敛阳而入阴 - Means 'only when sleeping can one gather yang and enter yin,' suggesting a way to reconcile the duality.
  • 可以与鬼相见 - Means 'one can meet with ghosts,' indicating a possible interaction.
  • 神虽遇而形不接 - Means 'the spirit meets but the form does not connect,' suggesting a separation between the spiritual and physical.
  • 乃无害也 - Means 'thus there is no harm,' indicating a resolution.
  • 夫卢充金碗 - Refers to a legendary story about Lu Chong and a golden bowl, indicating a historical or mythological reference.
  • 于古尝闻 - Means 'I have heard of it in ancient times,' indicating a historical context.
  • 宋玉瑶姬 - Refers to Song Yu and Yaoji, indicating a literary or historical reference.
  • 偶然一见 - Means 'a chance encounter,' suggesting a fleeting interaction.
  • 至于日日相觌皆在梦中 - Means 'as for daily encounters, they are all in dreams,' indicating a recurring dream pattern.
  • 则载籍之所希睹也 - Means 'which is rarely seen in historical records,' indicating rarity.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 卢充金碗 - Refers to a legendary story about Lu Chong and a golden bowl, indicating a historical or mythological reference.
  • 宋玉瑶姬 - Refers to Song Yu and Yaoji, indicating a literary or historical reference.
  • 丁亥春 - Refers to the spring of the year Ding Hai (a Chinese zodiac year), indicating a specific time period.
  • 辛卯春四年矣 - Refers to the spring of the year Xin Mao, four years later, indicating a specific time period.
  • 金碗 - Refers to a golden bowl, indicating a valuable object.
  • 瑶姬 - Refers to a legendary or literary figure, indicating a cultural reference.

Proper Nouns

  • 卢充 - The name of a historical or legendary figure.
  • 宋玉 - The name of a historical or literary figure.
  • 瑶姬 - The name of a legendary or literary figure.
  • 杨 - The name of a person, likely a friend or associate of the protagonist.

[430]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 清明上冢 - Traditional practice of visiting ancestral graves during the Qingming Festival.
  • 就人家求饮 - Seeking a drink from a household.
  • 态殊婉娈 - Describes the girl's appearance as exceptionally graceful and charming.
  • 邀共坐 - Inviting someone to sit together.
  • 意甚款洽 - Indicates a very warm and friendly atmosphere.
  • 已许嫁未 - Asking if the girl has already been betrothed.
  • 媒 - Refers to a matchmaker.
  • 面赧避入 - The girl blushed and retreated.
  • 呼之不出 - Unable to call her out again.
  • 越半载 - After half a year.
  • 纳币 - The formal exchange of gifts to confirm a marriage.
  • 于归 - A euphemism for a woman's marriage.
  • 错愕不知所对 - Feeling surprised and not knowing how to respond.
  • 十岁失母 - Losing her mother at the age of ten.
  • 鞠于外氏五六年 - Being raised by her maternal relatives for five or six years.
  • 姻未必成 - The marriage might not have been arranged.
  • 托形以联其好 - Referring to a ghost or spirit taking the form of a person to facilitate a marriage.
  • 不可理推 - Unexplainable by reason or logic.
  • 鬼魅 - Ghosts or spirits.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 清明上冢 - A significant cultural practice in Chinese tradition.
  • 媒 - The role of matchmakers in traditional Chinese society.
  • 纳币 - A formal step in traditional Chinese weddings.
  • 于归 - A traditional term for a woman's marriage.
  • 托形以联其好 - A concept from Chinese folklore where spirits or ghosts take human form to help arrange marriages.
  • 不可理推 - A phrase indicating that something is beyond logical explanation, often used in stories involving the supernatural.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '见女子立树下，态殊婉娈' describes the girl's appearance but could be interpreted as either a direct observation or a narrative description.
  • The sentence '仍邀共坐，意甚款洽' is elliptical and could be expanded to clarify the sequence of events.
  • The sentence '数月不见，汝更长成矣' is ambiguous about whether the speaker is referring to the girl's physical growth or maturity.
  • The sentence '于归后，媪抚其肩曰' is elliptical and could be expanded to clarify the sequence of actions.
  • The phrase '托形以联其好' is metaphorical and could be expanded to clarify the concept of spirits taking human form.
  • The phrase '不可理推' is abstract and could be expanded to clarify the idea of something being beyond logical explanation.

[431]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 善惡之無報 - Means 'the lack of retribution for good and evil,' expressing doubt about the concept of karma or justice.
  • 其间功过互偿 - Means 'there is mutual compensation for merits and faults.'
  • 或以无报为报 - Means 'sometimes what seems like no retribution is actually retribution.'
  • 罪福未尽，或有报而不即报 - Means 'if the sins and merits are not fully accounted for, sometimes there is retribution but it is not immediate.'
  • 毫厘比较，益微乎微矣 - Means 'the comparison is even finer than a hair's breadth.'
  • 君执目前所见，而疑天道难明 - Means 'you base your doubts on what you see now and question the clarity of heavenly justice.'
  • 岂不值乎 - Means 'isn't this unreasonable?'
  • 君命本当以流外出身，官至七品 - Means 'your fate was supposed to be through a minor official background, reaching a seventh-rank position.'
  • 以君机械多端，伺察多术 - Means 'because you are cunning and crafty, and skilled in espionage.'
  • 工于趋避，而深于挤排 - Means 'skilled in avoiding danger and adept at pushing others aside.'
  • 遂削官为八品 - Means 'you were demoted to an eighth-rank position.'
  • 迁八品之时，自谓以心计巧密，由九品而升 - Means 'when you were promoted to an eighth-rank position, you thought that your cleverness and subtlety had brought you from a ninth-rank position.'
  • 不知正以心计巧密，由七品而降也 - Means 'but you didn't realize that it was because of your cleverness and subtlety that you were demoted from a seventh-rank position.'
  • 因附耳而语，语乞大声曰 - Means 'he whispered something in your ear and then asked you to speak loudly.'
  • 君忘之乎 - Means 'have you forgotten?'
  • 岂独我知，三界孰不知 - Means 'not only do I know, but everyone in the three realms knows.'
  • 掉头上马 - Means 'he turned his head and mounted his horse.'
  • 惟见黄尘滚滚然，斯须灭迹 - Means 'all you could see was a cloud of yellow dust, and soon it disappeared.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 雍正癸丑 - Refers to the year 1733, a specific point in Qing dynasty history.
  • 会试 - A major imperial examination held in the capital, crucial for aspiring officials.
  • 白沟河 - A historical river in Hebei Province, known for its strategic importance.
  • 三界 - Refers to the three realms of existence in Buddhist cosmology: desire, form, and formlessness.
  • 因果有爽 - A reference to the Buddhist concept of karma, questioning the fairness of cause and effect.

Proper Nouns

  • 交河 - A place name, likely referring to a region in present-day Xinjiang, China.
  • 雍正 - The reign title of Emperor Yongzheng of the Qing dynasty.
  • 癸丑 - The year 1733, identified by the Chinese zodiac and stem-branch system.
  • 白沟河 - A river in Hebei Province, China.
  • 三界 - The three realms of existence in Buddhist cosmology: desire, form, and formlessness.

[432]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 乾隆壬戌癸亥間 - Refers to the years 1752 and 1753 during the Qianlong era, using the Chinese calendar system.
  • 尻骨生尾 - Describes an abnormal growth resembling a tail on the coccyx bone.
  • 如鹿角如珊瑚枝 - Metaphorical description of the growth's appearance.
  • 陰挺 - Refers to an abnormal protrusion from the genital area.
  • 如葡萄如芝菌 - Metaphorical description of the appearance of the protrusion.
  • 喧言有妖人投藥於井 - A rumor suggesting that a sorcerer poisoned wells with medicine.
  • 一割立愈 - Describes the immediate healing after surgical intervention.
  • 不醫則死 - Indicates that without treatment, the condition leads to death.
  • 鎮定 - Means to calm or stabilize a situation.
  • 縱奸 - Implies allowing or tolerating wrongdoing.
  • 金 - Refers to gold, specifically in the context of illegal mining.
  • 斷其糧道 - A strategy to cut off the supply lines of the miners.
  • 瞻言百里 - An idiom meaning to foresee far ahead, indicating foresight and wisdom.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 乾隆 - The reign title of Emperor Qianlong of the Qing dynasty.
  • 壬戌癸亥 - Chinese calendar years corresponding to 1752 and 1753.
  • 河間守 - Refers to the governor of Hejian (modern-day Hebei province).
  • 永公 - Refers to Yǒng, the cabinet scholar and governor of Hejian.
  • 乌鲁木齐 - A city in modern-day Xinjiang, China, historically known as Urumqi.
  • 深山中 - Refers to remote mountainous areas, often associated with illegal activities.
  • 金 - In the context of illegal mining, 'gold' refers to the precious metal.
  • 斷其糧道 - A military strategy used to cut off supplies to an enemy.
  • 瞻言百里 - An idiom indicating foresight and wisdom.

Proper Nouns

  • 乾隆 - The reign title of Emperor Qianlong.
  • 壬戌癸亥 - Chinese calendar years corresponding to 1752 and 1753.
  • 河間 - The place name Hejian, modern-day Hebei province.
  • 永公 - Yǒng, the cabinet scholar and governor of Hejian.
  • 乌鲁木齐 - The place name Urumqi, modern-day Xinjiang, China.
  • 金 - The precious metal gold.

[433]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 寓言 - A parable or allegory used to convey a moral lesson.
  • 负心 - Betrayal of trust or ingratitude.
  • 不烦驱策 - Does not require coercion or command.
  • 警夜不失职 - Stays vigilant and performs duties faithfully at night.
  • 恋主不他往 - Loyal to the master and does not wander elsewhere.
  • 实万万不能及 - Truly far beyond the capabilities of others.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 王征君载扬 - Wang Zaiyang, a respected scholar or official during the Qing dynasty.
  • 嘉祥曾映华 - Zeng Yinghua, a contemporary of Ji Yun, known for his commentary on *Yuewei Caotang Biji*.
  • 嘉祥 - A place name, likely referring to Jiaxiang, a county in Shandong Province.
  • 嘉祥曾映华曰 - The commentary by Zeng Yinghua, providing insight into the text's meaning and intent.
  • 犬之为物 - Refers to dogs, emphasizing their loyalty and moral qualities.
  • 僮仆 - Servants or attendants, often used in classical Chinese to describe household workers.
  • 偷儿 - Thieves or intruders.
  • 后垣半圮 - The back wall of the house was partially collapsed.
  • 事人之事 - Performing duties for one's master.

Proper Nouns

  • 阅微草堂笔记 - *Yuewei Caotang Biji*, a collection of notes by Ji Yun.
  • 如是我闻 - A common phrase in Buddhist texts.
  • 嘉祥曾映华 - Zeng Yinghua, a commentator on *Yuewei Caotang Biji*.
  • 王征君载扬 - Wang Zaiyang, a respected figure in the Qing dynasty.
  • 嘉祥 - Jiaxiang, a place name in Shandong Province.

[434]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 从孙翰清言 - Classical narrative device meaning 'according to the clear account of the grandson Han,' introducing the story.
  • 为狐所媚 - Means 'to be bewitched by a fox,' a common motif in Chinese folklore.
  • 附于其身 - Means 'to attach itself to his body,' indicating the fox's possession.
  • 恒在襟袂间与人语 - Means 'always in the folds of his clothes and speaking to people,' describing the fox's behavior.
  • 悬钟馗小像于壁 - Means 'to hang a small image of Zhong Kui on the wall,' Zhong Kui being a legendary demon-quelling figure.
  • 夜闻室中跳踯声 - Means 'to hear jumping sounds in the room at night,' indicating the fox's movement.
  • 诘以曾睹钟馗否 - Means 'to question him if he had ever seen Zhong Kui,' a rhetorical device to test the fox's reaction.
  • 钟馗甚可怖 - Means 'Zhong Kui is very terrifying,' emphasizing the fox's fear.
  • 幸其躯干仅尺余 - Means 'fortunately its body is only a foot long,' a playful remark about Zhong Kui's size.
  • 其剑仅数寸 - Means 'its sword is only a few inches long,' another playful remark.
  • 彼上床则我下床，彼下床则我上床 - Means 'when he goes to bed, I get down; when he gets down, I go to bed,' a humorous description of the fox's strategy.
  • 终不能击及我耳 - Means 'he cannot hit me at all,' a boastful statement by the fox.
  • 画像果有灵欤 - Means 'is the painting truly magical?' a rhetorical question.
  • 画像之灵，果躯干皆如所画欤 - Means 'is the magic of the painting truly as depicted?' another rhetorical question.
  • 设画为径寸之像，亦执针锋之剑，蠕蠕然而斩邪欤 - Means 'if the painting were a tiny image, would it also wield a needle-thin sword and gently cut away evil?' a whimsical thought experiment.
  • 是真不可解矣 - Means 'this is truly inexplicable,' a concluding remark.
  • 乾隆戊午夏 - Refers to the summer of 1788, a specific time period.
  • 献县修城役夫数百 - Means 'hundreds of laborers repairing the city wall in Xian County,' a specific location and event.
  • 破砖掷城下 - Means 'to throw broken bricks down to the base of the wall,' describing the labor process.
  • 鸣柝聚食 - Means 'to beat drums and gather for meals,' a common practice among laborers.
  • 顷运砖时 - Means 'just now while transporting bricks,' a specific moment in time.
  • 耳畔大声 - Means 'a loud voice by the ear,' a sudden and startling event.
  • 回顾无所睹 - Means 'looking back saw nothing,' indicating the mysterious nature of the voice.
  • 殊可怪也 - Means 'this is truly strange,' a comment on the unusual event.
  • 俄而众手合作 - Means 'soon everyone worked together,' a description of the sudden action.
  • 砖落如雹 - Means 'bricks fell like hail,' a vivid description of the accident.
  • 一砖适中辛五，脑裂死 - Means 'one brick hit Xin Wu, causing his skull to split and he died,' a tragic outcome.
  • 惊呼扰攘 - Means 'shouts of alarm and confusion,' describing the reaction.
  • 竟不得击者主名 - Means 'the name of the person who struck was never found,' indicating the mystery.
  • 官司莫能诘断 - Means 'the authorities could not investigate or judge,' a statement on the inability to solve the case.
  • 令役夫之长出钱十千，棺敛而已 - Means 'the labor supervisor was ordered to pay ten thousand coins for the coffin and burial,' a resolution to the incident.
  • 夙生负击者命 - Means 'Xin Wu's past life owed the striker a debt,' a reference to karma.
  • 夙生负辛五钱 - Means 'the striker's past life owed Xin Wu money,' another reference to karma.
  • 因果牵缠 - Means 'karma and fate entangled,' a philosophical concept.
  • 终相填补 - Means 'eventually they balanced each other out,' a conclusion on the karmic balance.
  • 微鬼神先告 - Means 'without the prior warning of ghosts and deities,' a statement on divine intervention.
  • 几何不以为偶然耶 - Means 'how could it not be seen as a coincidence?' a rhetorical question.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 博学求之，无不利于事也 - The closing clause links broad learning from such people to practical benefit in action.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 磷为鬼火 - A common Chinese belief that phosphorus is the cause of ghost fire, which is often observed in damp, marshy areas.
  • 博物志谓战血所成，非也 - A reference to the *Book of Miscellaneous Records* (博物志), an ancient Chinese encyclopedia, which incorrectly attributes ghost fire to battlefield blood.
  • 盖鬼者人之余气也 - A philosophical statement suggesting that ghosts are the residual energy of deceased humans.
  • 鬼属阴，而余气则属阳 - A statement about the nature of ghosts being yin (negative, passive) and the residual energy being yang (positive, active).
  • 阳为阴郁，则聚而成光 - A statement about the interaction between yin and yang, where yang energy can condense into light when it is blocked by yin.
  • 如雨气至阴，而萤火化；海气至阴，而阴火然也 - Analogies using rain and sea to illustrate the phenomenon of light formation from yin and yang.
  • 多见于秋冬而隐春夏 - Ghost fire is more commonly observed in autumn and winter and less so in spring and summer.
  • 秋冬气凝，春夏气散故也 - Explanation for the seasonal variation in ghost fire, attributing it to the condensation of qi (vital energy) in colder months and its dispersion in warmer months.
  • 其或见于春夏者，非幽房废宅，必深岩幽谷，皆阴气常聚故也 - Ghost fire may also appear in spring and summer, but only in places where yin energy is consistently present, such as abandoned houses or deep valleys.
  • 多在平原旷野，薮泽沮洳，阳寄于阴，地阴类，水亦阴类，从其本类故也 - Ghost fire is often found in open fields, wetlands, and marshes, where yang energy is trapped within yin environments.
  • 先兄晴湖，尝同沈丰功年丈夜行，而磷火在高树岭，青荧如炬，为从来所未闻 - A personal anecdote about the author's brother, Qinghu, and his friend, Shen Fenggong, encountering ghost fire on a high mountain ridge, described as a green, glowing light like a torch.
  • 李长吉诗曰：多年老県成木魅，笑声碧火巢中起 - A reference to a poem by Li He (李长吉), a Tang Dynasty poet, which describes old trees turning into wood spirits (木魅) and laughing ghostly fires rising from their nests.
  • 疑亦曾睹斯异，故有斯咏 - The author speculates that Li He might have witnessed this phenomenon, which inspired his poetic imagery.
  • 先兄所见或木魅所为欤 - The author wonders if his brother's experience was caused by wood spirits.

  • 后婴恶疾，楚毒万端，犹曰：此我诵咒未漱口，焚香用灶火，故得此报，非有他也 - She later fell ill with a severe disease, but still blamed it on minor infractions in her religious practices.
  • 愚哉！ - The monk concludes that she was foolish.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 如人世之有和息也 - A simile comparing the resolution of disputes to the concept of 'he-xi' (mediation or reconciliation) in human society.
  • 重业深仇 - Refers to grave offenses or deep-seated grudges.
  • 非人世所可和息者 - Means 'things that cannot be reconciled in human society.'
  • 非佛所能忏悔 - Means 'not something that can be repented of through Buddhist practices.'
  • 释迦牟尼亦无如之何 - Means 'even Shakyamuni Buddha has no power to deal with it,' emphasizing the limits of Buddhist influence.
  • 斯言平易而近理 - Means 'these words are simple and close to reason,' suggesting that the statement is straightforward and logical.
  • 儒者谓佛法为必无 - Means 'Confucian scholars say that Buddhism is definitely nonexistent,' reflecting a critical view of Buddhism.
  • 佛者谓种种罪恶皆可消灭 - Means 'Buddhists say that all kinds of sins can be eliminated,' reflecting a positive view of Buddhist practices.
  • 盖两失之 - Means 'both are wrong,' indicating that both views are flawed.
  • 余家距海仅百里 - Means 'my family lives only a hundred miles from the sea,' providing a geographical context.
  • 故河间古谓之瀛州 - Means 'therefore, the ancient name for Hejian was Yingzhou,' providing historical context.
  • 地势趋东，以渐而高 - Means 'the terrain slopes towards the east, gradually becoming higher,' describing the topography.
  • 海岸绝陡，潮不能出，水亦不能入 - Means 'the coastline is extremely steep, the tide cannot go out, and water cannot enter,' describing the coastal features.
  • 九河皆在河间 - Means 'all nine rivers are in Hejian,' referring to the ancient nine rivers in the region.
  • 大禹导河不直使入海 - Means 'the Great Yu did not directly lead the rivers into the sea,' referring to the legendary figure's efforts in river management.
  • 引之北行数百里，自碣石乃入 - Means 'he led them north for hundreds of miles, and they entered the sea at Jieshi,' providing details of the river management.
  • 海中每数岁或数十岁 - Means 'in the sea, every few years or several decades,' setting the time frame for the phenomenon.
  • 遥见水云眒洞中，红光烛天 - Means 'from afar, one can see water and clouds in a shimmering void, with red light illuminating the sky,' describing a spectacular natural phenomenon.
  • 谓之烧海 - Means 'it is called burning sea,' giving the name to the phenomenon.
  • 辄有断椽折栋，随潮而上 - Means 'there are often broken rafters and beams that follow the tide up,' describing the debris seen during the phenomenon.
  • 人取以为薪 - Means 'people pick them up as firewood,' indicating a practical use of the debris.
  • 越数日，必互言某匠某匠，为神召去营龙宫 - Means 'after a few days, people will often say that certain craftsmen were summoned by the gods to build the dragon palace,' describing a local legend.
  • 然无亲睹其人话鲛室贝阙之状者 - Means 'however, no one has personally seen someone describe the appearance of the dragon palace,' indicating the lack of firsthand accounts.
  • 第传闻而已 - Means 'it is only hearsay,' emphasizing the uncertain nature of the legend.
  • 殆重洋巨舶，弗戒于火，水光映射，空无障翳 - Means 'it is likely that large ships at sea caught fire, and the water's reflection illuminated the sky, with no obstacles in the air,' providing a scientific explanation for the phenomenon.
  • 梁柱之类，舶上皆有，亦不必定属殿材也 - Means 'beams and columns are found on ships, and they are not necessarily palace materials,' clarifying the nature of the debris.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 林塘知其异人 - 林塘 recognized him as an extraordinary person. The phrase '知其异人' is a classical way of expressing that someone is perceived as unusual or exceptional.
  • 因问以神仙感遇之事 - and asked him about matters of meeting with immortals. The phrase '神仙感遇' refers to encounters or experiences with immortals, a common theme in Chinese mythology and literature.
  • 有寓言者，有托名者，有借抒恩怨者，有喜谈诙诡以诧异闻者，有点缀风流，以为佳话，有本无所取而寄情绮语 - These phrases describe various types of stories or narratives found in classical texts. Each phrase is a distinct category of storytelling: '寓言者' - those who use allegory; '托名者' - those who use pseudonyms; '借抒恩怨者' - those who express grievances or grudges; '喜谈诙诡以诧异闻者' - those who enjoy talking about strange and fantastic tales; '点缀风流，以为佳话' - those who embellish tales of elegance and romance, making them into beautiful stories; '本无所取而寄情绮语' - those who have no real basis but express their feelings through beautiful language.
  • 大都伪者十八九，真者十一二 - Mostly, they are false (eighty-nine out of a hundred), and only a few are true (eleven out of a hundred). This is a classical way of expressing a high proportion of falsehoods and a low proportion of truths.
  • 此一二真者，又大都皆才鬼灵狐花妖木魅 - Among these few true ones, most are talented ghosts, fox spirits, flower fairies, and tree demons. These are supernatural beings often found in Chinese folklore.
  • 其称神仙必诡词 - Those who claim to be immortals must use deceptive words. The phrase '诡词' means 'deceptive or misleading words'.
  • 夫神正直而聪明，仙冲虚而清静 - Immortals are upright and wise, while gods are serene and tranquil. These phrases describe the characteristics of immortals and gods in Chinese mythology.
  • 岂有名列丹台，身依紫府，复有荡姬佚女，参杂其间，动入桑中之会哉 - How could there be immortals listed on the red platform, residing in the purple palace, with promiscuous women and idle maidens mixed among them, constantly engaging in licentious meetings? This is a rhetorical question expressing disbelief at the idea of immortals behaving in such a manner.
  • 林塘叹其精识，为古所未闻 - Lin Tang admired his profound insight, which was unheard of in ancient times. The phrase '精识' means 'profound insight or understanding'.
  • 说是事时，林塘未举其名字 - When he was telling this story, Lin Tang did not mention his name. The phrase '举其名字' means 'to mention his name'.
  • 后以问林塘子钟侨，钟侨曰：见此僧时，才五六岁，当时未闻呼名字，今无可问矣 - Later, he asked Lin Tang's son Zhong Qiao, who said: 'When I saw this monk, I was only five or six years old, and at that time, I did not hear his name mentioned. Now there is no one to ask.' The phrase '无可问矣' means 'there is no one to ask anymore'.

  • 丹台 - A mythical platform associated with immortals.
  • 紫府 - A mythical palace associated with immortals.
  • 荡姬佚女 - Refers to promiscuous women and idle maidens, often used in a derogatory sense.
  • 桑中之会 - Refers to licentious or improper meetings, often associated with immoral behavior.

  • 疑荡妇之赴幽期者 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'suspecting it was a loose woman meeting a secret lover,' implying a romantic or illicit encounter.
  • 逼往视之，寂无所睹 - Phrase meaning 'he went to see it, but found nothing,' indicating a sense of mystery or supernatural occurrence.
  • 疑其窜伏深丛，不复追迹 - Phrase meaning 'suspecting she had hidden herself in the deep underbrush, he no longer pursued her,' suggesting a decision to give up the search.

  • 我饿鬼也 - Phrase meaning 'I am a hungry ghost,' a reference to a supernatural being in Chinese folklore.
  • 以君有禄相，不敢触忤，故潜匿草间 - Phrase meaning 'because you have a fortune-teller's appearance, I dare not offend you, so I hid myself in the grass,' suggesting a supernatural being's respect for the protagonist.
  • 不虞忽相顾盼，枉步相寻，既尔有情，便当从君索食 - Phrase meaning 'I did not expect you to look at me, and to come to look for me, so now that we have feelings for each other, I will ask you for food,' indicating a supernatural being's request for sustenance.
  • 乞惠薄奠，即从此辞 - Phrase meaning 'I ask for a small offering, and then I will depart,' indicating a request for a ritual offering.
  • 其家为具纸钱肴酒，霍然而愈 - Phrase meaning 'his family prepared paper money, food, and wine, and he suddenly recovered,' indicating a ritual offering that restored the protagonist's health.
  • 苏进士语年曰 - Phrase meaning 'the scholar Su told me,' introducing a secondary source of information.
  • 此君本无邪心，以偶尔多事，遂为此鬼所乘 - Phrase meaning 'this gentleman had no evil intentions, but because he happened to be doing something, he was taken by this ghost,' suggesting a supernatural event.
  • 小人之于君子，恒伺隙而中之也，言动可不慎哉 - Phrase meaning 'the unscrupulous always look for opportunities to harm the virtuous, so one must be cautious in words and actions,' a moral lesson about vigilance.

  • 化生必产育，产育必秽污 - Birth must lead to reproduction, and reproduction must result in impurity.
  • 虽贤媛淑母亦不得不然 - Even virtuous and noble mothers cannot avoid this.
  • 非自作之罪也 - It is not a self-inflicted sin.
  • 如以为罪，则饮食不能不便溺 - If it is considered a sin, then eating and drinking cannot be done without excretion.
  • 口鼻不能不涕唾 - And the mouth and nose cannot avoid spitting and sneezing.
  • 是亦秽污，是亦当有罪乎 - These are also impurities, and should they also be considered sins?
  • 为是说者 - Those who say this.
  • 盖以最易惑者惟妇女 - Because the easiest to deceive are women.
  • 妇女所必不免者惟产育 - And what women cannot avoid is childbirth.
  • 以是为有罪，以是罪为非忏不可 - To consider this a sin, and to insist that this sin must be repented.
  • 而闺阁之财无不充功德之费矣 - And the wealth of the inner chambers is all used for meritorious deeds.
  • 尔出入冥司，宜有闻见 - You who come and go in the underworld should have seen and heard.
  • 血池果在何处 - Where is the blood pool indeed?
  • 堕血池者果有何人 - Who indeed falls into the blood pool?
  • 乃犹疑而问之欤 - Yet you still doubt and ask?
  • 走无常后以告人 - After the Messenger of Death, he told others.
  • 人讫无信其言者 - No one believed his words.
  • 积重不返 - The weight has accumulated and cannot be returned.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 帝王 - Refers to rulers or emperors.
  • 圣人 - Refers to sages or wise men.
  • 佛 - Refers to Buddhism.
  • 缁徒 - Refers to Buddhist monks.
  • 罪福之说 - Refers to the Buddhist doctrine of sin and merit.
  • 福田之说 - Refers to the Buddhist concept of 'fields of merit,' where good deeds are believed to produce spiritual rewards.
  • 瞿昙氏 - Refers to the Buddha, whose family name was Gautama (瞿昙 is the Chinese transliteration).
  • 无常 - Refers to the Buddhist concept of impermanence, and also the Messenger of Death.
  • 血盆忏经 - Refers to the 'Blood Bowl Repentance Sutra,' a Buddhist text.
  • 冥吏 - Refers to officials in the underworld.
  • 血池 - Refers to a place of punishment in the Buddhist underworld.
  • 闺阁 - Refers to the inner chambers of a woman's home, often associated with female virtue and wealth.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence compares the methods of rulers, sages, and Buddhists in encouraging good behavior, noting that while the methods differ, the intent is the same.
  • This sentence criticizes Buddhist monks for using the doctrine of sin and merit to coerce simple people, and for focusing on almsgiving rather than moral character, leading to the obscuring of the Buddha's original intent.
  • This sentence describes a person who travels with the Messenger of Death and asks an underworld official about the benefits of the Blood Bowl Repentance Sutra, to which the official replies that there is no such thing.
  • This sentence explains the natural process of birth and reproduction, arguing that it is not a sin.
  • This sentence criticizes those who argue that childbirth is a sin, and that this sin must be repented, leading to the use of women's wealth for meritorious deeds.
  • This sentence questions the existence of the blood pool and those who fall into it, suggesting that the person asking has not truly experienced the underworld.
  • This sentence concludes that after the Messenger of Death, the person told others, but no one believed his words, and the weight of the situation has accumulated and cannot be returned.

  • 则泥土杖画十六字曰 - Refers to the discovery of writing in the mud, meaning 'then they saw sixteen characters drawn with a stick in the mud.'
  • 不趁凉爽，自课生徒，溷入书馆，不亦愧乎 - Refers to the criticism of teaching in poor conditions, meaning 'not taking advantage of the coolness, teaching students, mixing into the school, is not this shameful?'
  • 盖祠无居人，狐据其中 - Refers to the situation of a fox living in the temple, meaning 'the temple had no residents, and a fox was living there.'
  • 怪二人久聒也 - Refers to the fox's annoyance, meaning 'the fox was annoyed by the two people talking for so long.'
  • 时程试方增律诗 - Refers to the context of the imperial examination, meaning 'at the time, the imperial examination was adding regulated verse.'
  • 飞万戏曰 - Refers to a playful remark, meaning 'Feiwan joked.'
  • 随手成文，即四言叶韵 - Refers to the writing style, meaning 'writing something offhand, even in four-character rhyming.'
  • 我愧此狐 - Refers to the author's feeling, meaning 'I am ashamed of this fox.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 飛萬又言 - Literally 'Fei Wan said again,' implies Fei Wan is making another statement or adding to a previous one.
  • 一書生最有膽 - Literally 'a scholar who is most courageous,' means 'a scholar who is very brave.'
  • 每求見鬼，不可得 - Literally 'always seeking to see ghosts, but never succeeding,' expresses repeated failure.
  • 一夕，雨霁月明 - Literally 'one night, after the rain had stopped and the moon was bright,' a poetic expression setting the scene.
  • 命小奴携罂酒诣丛冢间 - Literally 'ordered a small servant to carry a jar of wine to the cluster of tombs,' a vivid description of Fei Wan's actions.
  • 四顧呼曰 - Literally 'looked around and called out,' indicating a deliberate action.
  • 良夜獨遊，殊為寂寞 - Literally 'a good night for a solitary walk, but quite lonely,' a poetic expression of loneliness.
  • 泉下諸友，有肯來共酌者乎 - Literally 'friends in the netherworld, would any be willing to join in a drink with me?,' a respectful and somewhat humorous way of inviting ghosts to join in a drink.
  • 俄見磷光熒熒，出沒草際 - Literally 'soon saw flickering phosphorescent light, appearing and disappearing among the grass,' describes the appearance of ghosts.
  • 再呼之，嗚嗚相距丈許，皆止不進 - Literally 'called again, and they made a moaning sound, staying about a zhang away, all stopping and not advancing,' describes the reaction of the ghosts.
  • 數其影約十餘 - Literally 'counted their shadows, about ten or so,' conveys the number of ghosts.
  • 以巨杯挹酒，灑之 - Literally 'poured wine from a large cup, sprinkling it,' describes the act of offering wine to the ghosts.
  • 皆俯嗅其氣 - Literally 'all bent down to smell the aroma,' a vivid description of the ghosts' reaction to the wine.
  • 有一鬼稱酒絕佳，請再賜 - Literally 'one ghost praised the wine as excellent, asking for more,' shows the ghost's appreciation.
  • 因且灑且問曰 - Literally 'while sprinkling, he asked,' indicates a continuous action.
  • 何故不輪迴 - Literally 'why don't you reincarnate?,' a direct question about the ghosts' situation.
  • 善根在者轉生矣，惡貫盈者墮獄矣 - Literally 'those with good roots are reborn, those with evil deeds are sent to hell,' explains the fate of souls based on their actions.
  • 我輩十三人，罪根未滿，待輪迴者四；業報沉淪，不得輪迴者九也 - Literally 'we thirteen, our sins are not yet complete, four are waiting to be reborn; nine are bound by karma and cannot be reborn,' explains the specific situation of the ghosts.
  • 何不懺悔求解脫 - Literally 'why don't you repent and seek liberation?,' a question about the possibility of redemption.
  • 懺悔須及未死時，死後無着力處矣 - Literally 'repentance must be done before death, after death there is no place to exert effort,' emphasizes the importance of repenting while alive.
  • 灑酒既盡，舉罂視之，各踉蹌去 - Literally 'after the wine was finished, he raised the jar to look at them, and they staggered away,' describes the end of the interaction.
  • 中一鬼回首丁寧曰 - Literally 'one ghost turned back and said earnestly,' indicates a final message.
  • 餓鬼得飫壺觴，無以報德，謹以一語奉贈，懺悔須及未死時也 - Literally 'hungry ghosts who have been satisfied with wine, have nothing to repay the kindness, so I offer you a word of advice: repentance must be done before death,' the final message of the ghost.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 号呼越夕而死 - This phrase is a hyperbolic expression that needs to be rendered in a way that conveys the intensity of the action.
  • 偶为同事者所慢，因密讦其寝，盗阴事于上官 - This long sequence of actions must be read as a single narrative of events leading to a major case.
  • 竟成大狱，后自啮其舌而死 - The final outcome of the case and the subsequent death must be clearly linked.
  • 吴兴某以善治钱谷有声，偶为同事者所慢，因密讦其寝，盗阴事于上官，竟成大狱，后自啮其舌而死 - This long sentence must be broken down into readable English while preserving the sequence of events.
  • 无锡张某在归安令裘鲁青幕，有奸夫杀本夫者，裘以妇不同谋，欲出之，张大言曰 - This long sentence must be broken down into readable English while preserving the sequence of events.
  • 赵盾不讨贼为杀君，许止不尝药为弑父，春秋有诛意之法，是不可纵也 - This long sequence of historical and legal references must be read as a single argument.

  • 老儒掉头面壁曰 - Describes the scholar's response, turning away and facing the wall, indicating a refusal to answer.
  • 子不语怪 - A reference to Confucius' saying, 'The Master does not discuss the strange,' suggesting that the scholar believes such phenomena should not be discussed.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 姚安公 - A personal name, likely referring to a historical figure or a character in a literary work.
  • 舅氏陈公德音 - Refers to Yao An Gong's uncle, Tengong De Yin, a respected figure in the household.
  • 西席 - A term for a private tutor or teacher in a household.
  • 老儒 - Refers to an elderly scholar, often associated with Confucian learning.
  • 子不语怪 - A reference to Confucius' saying, which suggests that the scholar is invoking a principle of not discussing supernatural or unusual phenomena.
  • 沤麻水 - Water used to soak flax, which is unusual for rain.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence introduces the narrator and the setting.
  • This sequence describes the unusual rain, its duration, and its nature.
  • Introduces the scholar and the people's question.
  • The specific question asked.
  • Describes the scholar's response.
  • The scholar's reply, invoking Confucius' principle.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 曩客山西时 - 曩 means formerly or in the past, 客 means to be a guest or travel as a guest; indicates the speaker was once a guest or traveler in Shanxi.
  • 老儒 - Refers to an elderly scholar or Confucian scholar.
  • 经古冢 - 经 means to pass by or go through, 古冢 means ancient tomb; describes the scholar passing by an ancient tomb.
  • 詈之 - 詈 means to curse or scold.
  • 无他异 - 无 means no or not, 他异 means other strange occurrences; indicates there were no other unusual events.
  • 妻子不宿饱 - 妻子 means wife and children, 不宿饱 means do not have enough to eat for the night; indicates the scholar's family often goes hungry.
  • 铢积锱累 - 铢 and 锱 are ancient units of weight; means to accumulate wealth bit by bit.
  • 秘缄之 - 秘 means secretly, 缄 means to seal; indicates the scholar kept the gold secret and sealed it.
  • 对人自诉无担石 - 对人 means to others, 自诉 means to claim, 无担石 means without a single measure of grain; indicates the scholar claimed to have no wealth.
  • 忽置屋颠树杪 - 忽 means suddenly, 置 means to place, 屋颠 means on the roof, 树杪 means at the top of a tree; describes the sudden appearance of the gold on the roof or at the top of a tree.
  • 使梯而取 - 使 means to make someone, 梯 means ladder, 取 means to take; indicates the scholar had to use a ladder to retrieve the gold.
  • 忽在淤泥浅水 - 忽 means suddenly, 在 means in, 淤泥 means mud, 浅水 means shallow water; describes the sudden appearance of the gold in mud or shallow water.
  • 使濡而求 - 使 means to make someone, 濡 means to wet, 求 means to seek; indicates the scholar had to wade through water to retrieve the gold.
  • 忽投溷圊 - 忽 means suddenly, 投 means to throw, 溷圊 means latrine or toilet; describes the sudden appearance of the gold in the latrine.
  • 使探而濯 - 使 means to make someone, 探 means to reach into, 濯 means to wash; indicates the scholar had to reach into the latrine and wash the gold.
  • 或移易其地 - 或 means sometimes, 移易 means to move, 其地 means its place; indicates the gold sometimes moved to a different location.
  • 大索乃得 - 大索 means a thorough search, 乃得 means finally found; indicates the scholar had to conduct a thorough search to find the gold.
  • 或失去数日 - 或 means sometimes, 失去 means to lose, 数日 means several days; indicates the gold sometimes disappeared for several days.
  • 从空自堕 - 从空 means from the air, 自堕 means to fall down; describes the gold falling from the air.
  • 或与客对坐 - 或 means sometimes, 与 means with, 客 means guest, 对坐 means sitting opposite; indicates the gold sometimes appeared when the scholar was sitting opposite a guest.
  • 忽纳于帽檐 - 忽 means suddenly, 纳 means to put, 帽檐 means brim of the hat; describes the sudden appearance of the gold in the brim of the scholar's hat.
  • 或对人供揖 - 或 means sometimes, 对人 means to someone, 供 means to offer, 揖 means to bow; indicates the gold sometimes appeared when the scholar was bowing to someone.
  • 忽铿然脱袖 - 忽 means suddenly, 铿然 means with a clinking sound, 脱袖 means to fall out of the sleeve; describes the gold suddenly falling out of the scholar's sleeve with a clinking sound.
  • 千变万化 - 千变万化 means thousands of changes and transformations; indicates the unpredictable nature of the gold's movements.
  • 不可思议 - 不可 means cannot, 思议 means conceive or imagine; indicates the gold's movements are beyond comprehension.
  • 突四铤跃掷空中 - 突 means suddenly, 四铤 means four ingots, 跃 means to leap, 掷 means to throw; describes the four ingots suddenly leaping and flying through the air.
  • 如蛱蝶飞翔 - 如 means like, 蛱蝶 means butterfly, 飞翔 means to fly; compares the movement of the ingots to the flight of butterflies.
  • 弹丸击触 - 弹丸 means marble or small ball, 击触 means to hit and touch; describes the ingots hitting and touching each other.
  • 渐高渐远 - 渐 means gradually, 高 means high, 远 means far; indicates the ingots gradually rose higher and farther away.
  • 势将飞去 - 势 means tendency, 将 means about to, 飞去 means fly away; indicates the ingots were about to fly away.
  • 不得已，焚香拜祝 - 不得已 means out of necessity, 焚香 means to burn incense, 拜祝 means to bow and pray; indicates the scholar had to burn incense and pray out of necessity.
  • 始自投于怀 - 始 means finally, 自投 means to throw oneself, 于怀 means into the chest; indicates the ingots finally threw themselves into the scholar's chest.
  • 自是不复相嬲 - 自是 means from then on, 不复 means no longer, 相嬲 means to tease or bother each other; indicates the gold no longer bothered the scholar.
  • 讲学之气焰 - 讲学 means to teach or study, 之 means of, 气焰 means aura or manner; refers to the scholar's teaching demeanor.
  • 已索然尽矣 - 已 means already, 索然 means emptily or lacking, 尽矣 means ended; indicates the scholar's teaching demeanor had already ended.
  • 吾闻以德胜妖 - 吾闻 means I have heard, 以 means by, 德 means virtue, 胜妖 means to overcome evil spirits; indicates that the speaker has heard that virtue can overcome evil spirits.
  • 不闻以詈胜妖也 - 不闻 means I have not heard, 以 means by, 詈 means to curse, 胜妖 means to overcome evil spirits; indicates that the speaker has not heard that cursing can overcome evil spirits.
  • 其及也固宜 - 其 means it, 及 means to reach, 也 is a particle, 固宜 means naturally appropriate; indicates that it is naturally appropriate that the gold reached the scholar.
  • 使周张程朱詈 - 使 means if, 周张程朱 refers to the great Confucian scholars Zhou Dunyi, Zhang Zai, Cheng Hao, Cheng Yi, and Zhu Xi, 詈 means to curse; indicates if these scholars had cursed.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 性啬 - Compound term meaning 'frugal nature' or 'stingy disposition.'
  • 炊烟不举 - Literal meaning 'cooking smoke not raised,' indicating extreme poverty.
  • 乞贷三五金 - Literal meaning 'borrow three or five gold coins,' a request for a small sum of money.
  • 期明春以其夫馆谷偿 - Literal meaning 'hope to repay next spring with the grain from his husband's fields,' a promise to repay with future harvest.
  • 窘辞 - Literal meaning 'difficult words,' meaning an excuse or a refusal.
  • 脱簪珥付之去 - Literal meaning 'remove the hairpin and earrings and give them to her to leave,' an act of desperation to help the sister.
  • 盗穴壁入 - Literal meaning 'thief digs a hole in the wall to enter,' describing a method of burglary.
  • 罄所有去 - Literal meaning 'take everything away,' meaning the thief took all the possessions.
  • 迫于公论，弗敢告官捕 - Literal meaning 'forced by public opinion, dare not report to the authorities,' indicating the family's reluctance to involve the law.
  • 椎心茹痛 - Literal meaning 'pierce the heart and swallow the pain,' a vivid expression of deep sorrow.
  • 自匿其瑕 - Literal meaning 'hide one's own flaws,' meaning to conceal one's mistakes or weaknesses.
  • 颠倒播弄 - Literal meaning 'upset and manipulate,' describing the chaotic and cunning actions of the thief.
  • 若或使之哉 - Literal meaning 'as if someone is making it happen,' suggesting a sense of inevitability or fate.
  • 足为善 - Literal meaning 'worthy of goodness,' meaning the man's shame is a positive moral reaction.
  • 充此一愧 - Literal meaning 'fill this one shame,' suggesting that the man's shame could be expanded into a broader sense of moral improvement.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 孝廉 - Title for a candidate for the imperial examination, reflecting the Confucian value of filial piety and moral integrity.
  • 性啬 - Reflects a cultural value of frugality, but in this context, it is portrayed negatively.
  • 炊烟不举 - A common idiom in Chinese literature to describe extreme poverty.
  • 乞贷三五金 - A small sum of money, reflecting the economic conditions of the time.
  • 期明春以其夫馆谷偿 - A promise to repay with future harvest, indicating a common practice of deferred payment.
  • 窘辞 - Reflects the social norms of polite refusal in Chinese culture.
  • 脱簪珥付之去 - An act of desperation, reflecting the sister's poverty and the brother's initial unwillingness to help.
  • 盗穴壁入 - A method of burglary, reflecting the security concerns of the time.
  • 迫于公论，弗敢告官捕 - Reflects the importance of social harmony and the reluctance to involve the law.
  • 椎心茹痛 - A vivid expression of deep sorrow, reflecting the emotional impact of the theft.
  • 自匿其瑕 - Reflects the cultural value of maintaining face and avoiding shame.
  • 颠倒播弄 - Reflects the cunning and chaotic nature of the thief, a common theme in Chinese literature.
  • 若或使之哉 - Suggests a sense of inevitability or fate, reflecting the deterministic worldview of the time.
  • 足为善 - Reflects the Confucian value of moral improvement through self-reflection.
  • 充此一愧 - Suggests the potential for moral growth and improvement.

Proper Nouns

  • 香畹 - A name, possibly referring to a person or place.
  • 孝廉 - A title for a candidate for the imperial examination.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 誤延 - Literally 'mistakenly invite,' meaning to consult the wrong doctor or practitioner.
  • 讀景岳全書者 - Refers to someone who has studied or practiced according to the medical theories in *The Complete Works of Jingyue* by Zhang Jiebin.
  • 投人参 - Literally 'administered ginseng,' referring to the administration of ginseng as a remedy.
  • 立卒 - Literally 'died immediately,' indicating sudden death.
  • 哭极恸然 - Literally 'cried extremely mournfully,' describing intense grief.
  • 每一发声，辄闻板壁格格响 - Vivid description of the mother’s crying, where the sound of her voice causes the wooden walls to creak or crack.
  • 夜或绕床呼阿母 - Literally 'at night, she would sometimes walk around the bed calling 'Mother,' describing the mother’s restless behavior.
  • 灼然辨为霁渔声 - Literally 'clearly recognized as the voice of Jiyu,' indicating that the mother’s perception of the voice was unmistakable.
  • 盖不欲高年之过哀也 - Literally 'it is said that this was to prevent excessive grief in old age,' suggesting that the supernatural intervention was meant to protect the mother from overburdening her with sorrow.

  • 景岳全书 - Famous medical text written by Zhang Jiebin during the Ming Dynasty, known for its emphasis on the use of tonics and herbs like ginseng.
  • 人参 - Ginseng, a highly valued medicinal herb in traditional Chinese medicine, often used to strengthen the body but can be harmful if misused.
  • 太夫人 - Respectful title for a mother, often used in official documents or family records.
  • 高年之过哀 - Refers to the potential harm that excessive grief can cause to elderly people, a common theme in Chinese literature and philosophy.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 海阳鞠前辈庭和言 - Formal introduction, meaning 'Mr. Ju Tinghe, a respected elder from Haian, said.' Sets the narrative tone and introduces the speaker.
  • 一宦家妇临卒 - Literally 'a lady from an official family on her deathbed,' referring to a woman from a prominent family who is dying.
  • 左手挽幼儿，右手挽幼女 - Describes the woman holding a young boy and a young girl, respectively, with both hands. The word '挽' can also mean 'to pull' or 'to hold tightly,' emphasizing her emotional attachment.
  • 呜咽而终 - Means 'she wept and died,' indicating a scene of deep sorrow and emotional struggle.
  • 力擘之乃释 - Literally 'only after forcefully breaking (her grip) was she released,' suggesting the intensity of her grip and the difficulty in separating her from the children.
  • 目炯炯尚不瞑也 - Means 'her eyes were still wide open and did not close,' indicating that she died with her eyes open, symbolizing unresolved emotions or unfinished business.
  • 后灯前月下 - Poetic phrase meaning 'afterwards, under the light of lamps and moon,' setting a mysterious and eerie atmosphere.
  • 往往遥见其形 - Means 'she would often be seen at a distance,' implying that the woman's spirit appeared intermittently.
  • 呼之不应，问之不言，招之不来，即之不见 - Series of parallel phrases describing the ghostly figure's behavior: 'calling her did not respond, asking her did not answer, calling her did not come, and approaching her did not see her.' Emphasizes the ghost's elusive and unresponsive nature.
  • 或数夕不出，或一夕数出 - Means 'sometimes she would not appear for several nights, and sometimes she would appear multiple times in one night,' indicating the unpredictable nature of her appearances.
  • 或望之在某人前，而某人反无睹 - Means 'sometimes she was seen in front of someone, but that person did not see her,' suggesting that the ghost's presence was selective and not visible to everyone.
  • 或此处方睹，而彼处又睹 - Means 'sometimes she was seen in one place, and then in another place,' further emphasizing the ghost's elusive and unpredictable nature.
  • 如泡影空花，电光石火，一转瞬而即灭，一弹指而倏生 - Series of metaphors comparing the ghost's appearance to fleeting phenomena: 'like bubbles, like empty flowers, like lightning, like flashes of light, disappearing in an instant and reappearing in a blink.' Emphasizes the ghost's transient and ephemeral nature.
  • 虽不为害，而人人意中有一先亡夫人在 - Means 'although she did no harm, everyone felt as if the deceased lady was still present in their minds,' indicating the psychological impact of the ghost's presence.
  • 故后妻视其子女，不敢生分别心 - Means 'therefore, the stepmother did not dare to have any feelings of differentiation towards the children,' suggesting that the ghost's presence influenced the stepmother's behavior towards the children.
  • 婢媪僮仆，视其子女，亦不敢生凌侮心 - Means 'the maids, servants, and attendants also did not dare to have any feelings of contempt or disrespect towards the children,' indicating that the ghost's presence affected the household staff's behavior as well.
  • 至男婚女嫁，乃渐不睹 - Means 'when the children reached adulthood and got married, the ghost gradually stopped appearing,' suggesting that the ghost's presence diminished over time.
  • 然越数载，或一见 - Means 'but after several years, she might appear once,' indicating that the ghost's appearances became even rarer.
  • 故一家恒栗栗危惧，如在其旁 - Means 'therefore, the entire family always lived in constant fear, as if she were always nearby,' emphasizing the lasting psychological impact of the ghost's presence.
  • 或疑为狐魅所托 - Means 'some suspected that she was a fox spirit or a ghost,' suggesting alternative explanations for the ghost's behavior.
  • 殆结恋之极，精灵不散 - Means 'it seems that the extreme attachment of her spirit did not dissipate,' providing a possible explanation for the ghost's persistence.
  • 为人子女者，知父母之心，殁而弥切如是也 - Means 'those who were her children understood their parents' hearts, and their love for her became even stronger after her death,' emphasizing the deep emotional bond between the children and their mother.
  • 其亦可以怆然感乎 - Means 'one can also feel a sense of sorrow and emotion,' inviting the reader to reflect on the emotional impact of the story.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 鞠前辈庭和 - Mr. Ju Tinghe, a respected elder from Haian, is the narrator of the story. His status as a respected elder adds credibility to the account.
  • 宦家 - Refers to an official family, indicating that the woman came from a prominent and influential background.
  • 狐魅 - Refers to fox spirits or ghosts, which are common figures in Chinese folklore and often associated with supernatural phenomena.
  • 先亡夫人 - Refers to a deceased lady, emphasizing the woman's status and the respect she commanded even in death.

  • The clause '访问邻家，曰：是宅久空，是家来住仅月余，一夕自去，莫知所往矣' records the protagonist's inquiry and the neighbors' responses.
  • The clause '或曰狐也，小说中盖尝有是事。或曰是女为饵，窃赀远遁，伪为狐也' presents two alternative explanations for the woman's disappearance.
  • The clause '夫狐而伪人，斯亦黠矣；人而为狐，不更黠乎哉' evaluates the cunning of both the fox and the human.
  • The clause '余居京师五六十年，见此类者不胜数，此其一耳' concludes with the narrator's reflection on the frequency of such events.

  • 尽复所蓄药于水 - Means to throw all the stored love pills into water.
  • 燥烈之药 - Refers to dry and hot medicines.
  • 加以锻炼 - Means to refine or process the medicines further.
  • 其力既猛，其毒亦深 - Means that the stronger the medicine, the more toxic it is.
  • 吾见败事者多矣 - Means that the speaker has seen many failures caused by such medicines.
  • 退之硫黄，贤者不免 - Refers to the idea that even wise people can be affected by powerful medicines.
  • 殆数不应尽，故鉴于鼠而忽悟欤 - Means that perhaps the friend was not meant to die, so he suddenly understood the danger after seeing the mouse.

  • 硫黄 - Refers to sulfur, a common ingredient in traditional Chinese medicine, often used in aphrodisiacs.
  • 贤者 - Refers to wise or virtuous people, a common theme in classical Chinese texts.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 传闻异词 - Indicates that different people give different accounts of the same matter.
  • 其间颇有所未明 - Suggests that there are many things that are not fully understood or clear.
  • 闻君豁达，不自讳 - Implies that the person being addressed is open-minded and does not hide anything.
  • 故请祛所惑 - Means 'therefore, I ask to dispel my doubts.'
  • 天生万物，各命以名 - A philosophical statement meaning that all things created by heaven are given names.
  • 狐名狐，正如人名人耳 - A metaphor comparing the naming of foxes to the naming of humans, emphasizing the naturalness of such distinctions.
  • 呼狐为狐，正如呼人为人耳，何讳之有 - A rhetorical question suggesting that there is no need to be ashamed of calling a fox a fox, just as there is no need to be ashamed of calling a human a human.
  • 好眫不一，亦如人类之内良莠不齐 - Compares the diversity of foxes to the diversity of humans, indicating that both have good and bad individuals.
  • 人不讳人之恶，狐何讳狐之恶乎 - Another rhetorical question suggesting that if humans do not hide their own faults, why should foxes hide theirs?
  • 第言无隐 - A polite way of saying 'please speak frankly.'
  • 师退问狐有别乎 - A question asking whether there are different types of foxes.
  • 凡狐皆可以修道，而最灵者曰狴狐 - States that all foxes can cultivate the Dao, but the most intelligent ones are called *bi* foxes.
  • 此如农家读书者少，儒家读书者多也 - A comparison indicating that just as there are fewer farmers who read books and more Confucian scholars who do, there are fewer ordinary foxes and more intelligent ones.
  • 肶狐生而皆灵乎 - Asks whether all *bi* foxes are born with intelligence.
  • 此系乎其种类，未成道者所生则为常狐，已成道者所生，则自能变化也 - Explains that this depends on their species; those born to unenlightened foxes remain ordinary, while those born to enlightened foxes can transform themselves.
  • 既成道矣，自必驻颜，而小说载狐亦有翁媪，何也 - Questions why, if foxes have achieved the Dao and thus should retain their youth, some stories still depict old foxes.
  • 所谓成道，成人道也 - Clarifies that achieving the Dao means achieving human-like qualities.
  • 其饮食男女，生老病死，亦与人同 - Indicates that these foxes experience the same life cycle as humans.
  • 若夫飞升霞举，又自一事 - Refers to a different aspect of achieving the Dao, such as ascending to the heavens.
  • 此如千百人中，有一二人求仕宦，其炼形服气者，如积学以成名，其媚惑采补者，如捷径以求售 - Uses a metaphor to explain that among many foxes, some pursue enlightenment through disciplined practice (like studying to gain fame), while others use charm and seduction as a shortcut (like taking a quick path to success).

  • 飞升霞举 - Refers to the act of ascending to the heavens, a common theme in Chinese mythology and Daoist beliefs.
  • 炼形服气 - Refers to the practice of alchemy and breathing exercises to achieve spiritual transformation.
  • 媚惑采补 - Refers to the practice of seduction and charm, often associated with foxes in folklore.
  • 仕宦 - Refers to entering government service or achieving a high social status.
  • 积学以成名 - Refers to gaining fame through diligent study and hard work.
  • 捷径以求售 - Refers to taking a shortcut to achieve success.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 問或居人家，或居旷野 - Whether living in a house or in the wilderness, introducing a choice between two lifestyles.
  • 未成道者，未离乎兽 - Metaphorical expression meaning those who have not yet attained enlightenment are still like animals, implying a state of ignorance or lack of refinement.
  • 利于远人，非山林弗便也 - It is beneficial to live far from people, and nothing but the mountains and forests are convenient, emphasizing the isolation needed for spiritual growth.
  • 已成道者，事事与人同 - Those who have attained enlightenment live in harmony with others, suggesting a return to normalcy after spiritual achievement.
  • 利于近人，非城市弗便也 - It is beneficial to live close to people, and nothing but cities are convenient, indicating the need for social interaction post-enlightenment.
  • 其道行高者，则城市山林皆可居 - Those with high spiritual attainment can live in both cities and mountains, suggesting a level of spiritual mastery that transcends physical location.
  • 大富大贵家，其力百物皆可致 - Wealthy and noble families, with their power, can obtain everything, highlighting the material capabilities of the elite.
  • 住荒村僻壤与通都大邑 - Living in remote villages or bustling metropolises, contrasting two extremes of living conditions.
  • 一也 - It is the same, suggesting that the choice between the two lifestyles is ultimately indifferent.
  • 纵谈其大旨 - To freely discuss the main points, indicating an open and unrestricted conversation.
  • 劝人学道 - To encourage people to study the Way, referring to spiritual or philosophical teachings.
  • 吾曹辛苦一二百年，始化人身 - We have struggled for hundreds of years to transform into human form, indicating the long and arduous process of spiritual evolution.
  • 公等现是人身，功成已抵大半 - You are now in human form, and your progress is nearly complete, suggesting that the majority of the work has already been done.
  • 悠悠忽忽，与草木同朽 - Living aimlessly and eventually decaying like plants, implying a life without purpose or direction.
  • 殊可惜也 - It is truly a pity, expressing regret over wasted potential.
  • 师退腹笥三藏 - The master has a vast knowledge of the Tripitaka, indicating a deep understanding of Buddhist scriptures.
  • 佛家地位绝高 - The status of Buddhism is extremely high, emphasizing the elevated position of the religion.
  • 然或修持未到，一入轮回，便迷却本来面目 - However, if one's practice is not sufficient, once reborn, they will lose their true nature, warning against the dangers of incomplete spiritual practice.
  • 不如且求不死，为有把握 - It is better to seek immortality first, as it is more reliable, suggesting a pragmatic approach to spiritual goals.
  • 吾亦屡逢善知识，不敢见异而迁也 - I have often met virtuous teachers, and I dare not change my path because of novelty, indicating a commitment to one's spiritual path despite external influences.
  • 师退临别曰 - The master said upon parting, introducing a final message.
  • 今日相逢，亦是天幸 - Today's meeting is also a matter of good fortune, expressing gratitude for the encounter.
  • 君有一言赠我乎 - Do you have a word of advice for me? asking for guidance.
  • 踌躇良久曰 - After hesitating for a long time, he said, indicating a thoughtful response.
  • 三代以下，恐不好名 - Since the Three Dynasties, one might not seek fame, suggesting a shift in values.
  • 此为下等人言 - This is what I say to the lower class, indicating a different standard for different people.
  • 自古圣贤，却是心气和平 - Since ancient times, the sages have been calm and composed, emphasizing the ideal of inner peace.
  • 无一毫做作 - Without the slightest pretense, suggesting authenticity.
  • 洛闽诸儒，撑眉弩目 - The scholars of Lu and Min, with their raised brows and glaring eyes, describing a confrontational and aggressive attitude.
  • 便生出如许葛藤 - They create such tangles, implying unnecessary complications.
  • 先生其念之 - Please remember this, sir, giving a final piece of advice.
  • 盖师退崖岸太峻，时或过当云 - Because the master's demeanor was too severe, he sometimes went too far, providing a commentary on the master's character.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 成道 - Achieving enlightenment or spiritual perfection.
  • 山林 - Remote, natural settings often associated with meditation and spiritual retreat.
  • 城市 - Urban areas, often associated with social interaction and material life.
  • 大富大贵家 - Wealthy and noble families, often associated with power and influence.
  • 百物皆可致 - The ability to obtain anything, often associated with wealth and resources.
  • 荒村僻壤 - Remote and isolated villages, often associated with simplicity and seclusion.
  • 通都大邑 - Major cities, often associated with prosperity and activity.
  • 纵谈 - Free and open discussion, often associated with intellectual and philosophical discourse.
  • 学道 - The study of spiritual or philosophical teachings.
  • 佛家 - Buddhism, a major world religion.
  • 轮回 - The cycle of rebirth, a central concept in Buddhism.
  • 本来面目 - One's true nature or original self.
  • 不死 - Immortality, often associated with spiritual or physical longevity.
  • 善知识 - Virtuous teachers or guides in spiritual practice.
  • 洛闽 - The regions of Lu and Min, often associated with Confucian scholars.
  • 诸儒 - Various scholars or intellectuals.
  • 撑眉弩目 - A descriptive phrase indicating a confrontational and aggressive demeanor.
  • 葛藤 - Tangles or complications, often used metaphorically.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This question introduces a choice between two lifestyles and asks for the reason behind it.
  • This clause explains why isolation is beneficial for those who have not yet attained enlightenment.
  • This clause explains why social interaction is beneficial for those who have attained enlightenment.
  • This clause suggests that those with high spiritual attainment can live anywhere.
  • This clause contrasts the material capabilities of the elite with the choice of living conditions.
  • This clause introduces the master's message of encouraging spiritual study.
  • This clause describes the long process of spiritual evolution and the current state of progress.
  • This clause expresses regret over a life without purpose.
  • This clause describes the master's deep knowledge and the conversation about Zen.
  • This clause warns against the dangers of incomplete spiritual practice.
  • This clause suggests a pragmatic approach to spiritual goals.
  • This clause indicates a commitment to one's spiritual path.
  • This clause introduces a final message of gratitude.
  • This clause asks for guidance.
  • This clause provides a thoughtful response about the changing values.
  • This clause emphasizes the ideal of inner peace and authenticity.
  • This clause criticizes the aggressive attitudes of some scholars.
  • This clause provides a commentary on the master's character.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 不亦傎乎 - Rhetorical question meaning 'Isn't this foolish?' or 'Isn't this a mistake?' Used to express disbelief or criticism.
  • 岂但此辈痴 - Phrase meaning 'Not only are these people foolish,' indicating that the speaker believes there are others who are equally or more foolish.
  • 即彼建首善书院者，亦复大痴 - Phrase meaning 'Even those who built the Shou Shan Academy are extremely foolish,' suggesting that the speaker finds the actions of the academy builders to be equally foolish.
  • 奸珰柄国 - Phrase meaning 'Evil eunuchs hold power in the state,' referring to corrupt officials who have taken control of the government.
  • 方阴伺君子之隙 - Phrase meaning 'They secretly look for opportunities to attack virtuous men,' indicating that the eunuchs are plotting against virtuous officials.
  • 肆其诋排 - Phrase meaning 'They openly criticize and attack,' suggesting that the eunuchs are actively undermining virtuous officials.
  • 群聚清谈 - Phrase meaning 'They gather to engage in idle talk,' indicating that the virtuous officials are discussing abstract ideas rather than taking action.
  • 反予以钩党之题目 - Phrase meaning 'Instead, they are given the label of 'party' or 'faction,' suggesting that the virtuous officials are being accused of forming a political group.
  • 一网打尽 - Phrase meaning 'Caught in one net,' indicating that the virtuous officials are being eliminated all at once.
  • 何尤 - Phrase meaning 'What blame,' suggesting that there is no reason to criticize the eunuchs.
  • 三千弟子 - Phrase referring to the 3,000 disciples of Confucius, indicating a large group of followers.
  • 惟孔子则可 - Phrase meaning 'Only Confucius could do it,' suggesting that Confucius was unique in his ability to lead such a large group.
  • 孟子揣不及孔子 - Phrase meaning 'Mencius thought he could not match Confucius,' indicating that Mencius believed he was not as capable as Confucius.
  • 所与讲肄者，公孙丑万章等数人而已 - Phrase meaning 'The ones he taught were only a few, such as Gong Sun Chou and Wan Zhang,' indicating that Mencius had a smaller group of disciples.
  • 洛闽诸儒 - Phrase referring to the scholars of Lu and Min, indicating a group of Confucian scholars.
  • 无孔子之道德 - Phrase meaning 'Without Confucius's moral character,' suggesting that these scholars lacked the moral integrity of Confucius.
  • 招聚生徒，盈千累万 - Phrase meaning 'They gathered thousands and tens of thousands of students,' indicating a large number of followers.
  • 枭鸾并集 - Phrase meaning 'Eagles and phoenixes gather together,' suggesting a mix of good and bad people.
  • 门户交争 - Phrase meaning 'Factions compete with each other,' indicating internal conflicts among the scholars.
  • 遂酿为朋党 - Phrase meaning 'Thus, they formed factions,' suggesting that the internal conflicts led to the formation of political groups.
  • 国随以亡 - Phrase meaning 'And the state perished,' indicating that the formation of factions led to the downfall of the state.
  • 东林诸儒不鉴覆辙 - Phrase meaning 'The Donglin scholars did not learn from past mistakes,' suggesting that they repeated the same errors.
  • 鹜虚名而受实祸 - Phrase meaning 'They pursued empty fame and suffered real disasters,' indicating that their pursuit of reputation led to actual harm.
  • 凭吊遗踪 - Phrase meaning 'To visit the remains of the past,' suggesting a visit to the site of former events.

  • 曩遇一大家放焰口 - Once, I encountered a wealthy family performing the ritual of offering food to spirits.
  • 欲伺其匆扰取事 - I wanted to see if there was an opportunity to steal something when they were busy.
  • 乃无隙可乘 - There was no opportunity to take advantage of the situation.
  • 伏卧高楼檐角上 - I hid on the eaves of a tall building.
  • 俯见摇铃诵咒时 - From below, I saw the ringing of bells and chanting of mantras.
  • 有黑影无数，高可二三尺 - There were countless black shadows, about two or three feet tall.
  • 或逾垣入，或由窦入 - Some climbed over the walls, others entered through the holes.
  • 往来摇漾 - They moved back and forth, swaying.
  • 凡无人处皆满 - All empty places were filled.
  • 迨撒米时，倏聚倏散，倏前倏后 - When the rice was scattered, they suddenly gathered and dispersed, moving forward and backward.
  • 如环绕攘夺 - Like circling and fighting over the food.
  • 并仰接俯拾之态 - And the postures of reaching up and picking up the food.
  • 并仿佛依稀 - And vaguely visible.
  • 其色如轻烟 - Their color was like light smoke.
  • 其状略似人形 - Their shape was roughly human-like.
  • 但不辨五官四体耳 - But their facial features and limbs could not be distinguished.
  • 然则鬼犹求食，不信有之乎？ - Therefore, if ghosts still seek food, how can one not believe in their existence?

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 僧所诵焰口经 - Refers to the *Yan Kou Jing* (Fasting Mouth Sutra), a Buddhist text used in rituals to offer food to spirits.
  • 召魂施食诸梵咒 - Refers to the use of mantras to summon and feed spirits, a common practice in Buddhist rituals.
  • 印房官奴白六 - Refers to Bai Liu, a former notorious bandit who was later assigned to work in the printing office.

  • 撒米 - Refers to the act of scattering rice, a common ritual practice to feed spirits.
  • 仰接俯拾 - Refers to the postures of reaching up and picking up food, describing the actions of the spirits.
  • 轻烟 - Refers to the color of the spirits, which is described as resembling light smoke.
  • 人形 - Refers to the shape of the spirits, which is roughly human-like.
  • 五官四体 - Refers to facial features and limbs, indicating the difficulty in distinguishing the spirits' physical characteristics.
  • 鬼犹求食 - Refers to the belief that ghosts still seek food, supporting the existence of spirits.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence introduces the sutra and its characteristics, followed by a statement about the authenticity of the mantras.
  • This sentence sets the scene and introduces the debate about the matter.
  • This long, complex sentence narrates Bai Liu's experience and his observations of the spirits during the ritual.
  • This rhetorical question concludes the narrative and emphasizes the existence of ghosts.

  • 破窗纸窃窥，见二人攘臂奋击 - She broke the window paper to secretly look and saw two people with raised arms fighting. '破窗纸' means 'broke the window paper,' '窃窥' means 'secretly looked,' '见' means 'saw,' '二人' means 'two people,' '攘臂' means 'with raised arms,' and '奋击' means 'fighting.'
  • 一老翁举杖隔之不能止 - An old man raised a staff to separate them but could not stop them. '一老翁' means 'an old man,' '举杖' means 'raised a staff,' '隔之' means 'to separate them,' and '不能止' means 'could not stop.'
  • 俄相搏仆地，并现形为狐 - Suddenly, they fought and fell to the ground, and both revealed themselves as foxes. '俄' means 'suddenly,' '相搏' means 'fought,' '仆地' means 'fell to the ground,' '并现形' means 'both revealed themselves,' and '为狐' means 'as foxes.'
  • 跳踉摆拨，触老翁亦仆 - They jumped and struggled, knocking the old man down as well. '跳踉' means 'jumped,' '摆拨' means 'struggled,' '触' means 'knocked,' '老翁' means 'the old man,' and '亦仆' means 'as well fell.'
  • 老翁蹶起，一手按一狐 - The old man got up again and held one fox with one hand. '蹶起' means 'got up again,' '一手' means 'one hand,' and '按一狐' means 'held one fox.'
  • 呼曰：逆子不孝，朱五嫂可助我 - He shouted, 'Wicked son, you are ungrateful. Zhu's fifth wife can help me.' '呼曰' means 'shouted,' '逆子不孝' means 'wicked son, you are ungrateful,' and '朱五嫂可助我' means 'Zhu's fifth wife can help me.'
  • 朱伏不敢出，老翁顿足曰：当诉诸土神 - Zhu crouched in fear and did not dare to come out. The old man stomped his foot and said, 'I will report this to the earth god.' '朱伏不敢出' means 'Zhu crouched in fear and did not dare to come out,' '老翁顿足' means 'the old man stomped his foot,' and '当诉诸土神' means 'I will report this to the earth god.'
  • 恨恨而散 - He left in anger. '恨恨' means 'in anger' and '而散' means 'left.'
  • 次夜闻满园铃铛声，似有所搜捕 - The next night, she heard the sound of bells ringing all over the garden, as if there was a search. '次夜' means 'the next night,' '闻' means 'heard,' '满园铃铛声' means 'the sound of bells ringing all over the garden,' and '似有所搜捕' means 'as if there was a search.'
  • 觉几上瓦瓶似微动，怪而视之 - She felt the clay bottle on the table move slightly and looked at it in surprise. '觉' means 'felt,' '几上瓦瓶' means 'the clay bottle on the table,' '似微动' means 'move slightly,' '怪而视之' means 'looked at it in surprise.'
  • 瓶中小语曰：乞勿言，当报恩 - The bottle whispered, 'Please do not speak. I will repay the favor.' '瓶中' means 'in the bottle,' '小语' means 'whispered,' '乞勿言' means 'please do not speak,' and '当报恩' means 'I will repay the favor.'
  • 朱怒曰：父母恩且不肯报，何有于我 - Zhu angrily said, 'If you do not repay the favor to your parents, how can you repay me?' '朱怒曰' means 'Zhu angrily said,' '父母恩且不肯报' means 'If you do not repay the favor to your parents,' and '何有于我' means 'how can you repay me?'
  • 举瓶掷门外碑趺上，訇然而碎 - He threw the bottle onto the stone base of the monument outside the door, and it shattered with a loud noise. '举瓶' means 'threw the bottle,' '掷门外碑趺上' means 'onto the stone base of the monument outside the door,' and '訇然而碎' means 'shattered with a loud noise.'
  • 即闻噭噭有声，意其就执矣 - Immediately, he heard a sound of distress, thinking that the fox had been captured. '即闻' means 'immediately heard,' '噭噭有声' means 'a sound of distress,' and '意其就执矣' means 'thinking that the fox had been captured.'
  • 一轿夫曰：斗触父母倒，是何大事，乃至为土神捕捉，殊可怖也 - One sedan chair bearer said, 'Fighting and knocking down one's parents, what a big deal, and even being captured by the earth god, it is truly terrifying.' '一轿夫' means 'one sedan chair bearer,' '斗触父母倒' means 'fighting and knocking down one's parents,' '是何大事' means 'what a big deal,' '乃至为土神捕捉' means 'even being captured by the earth god,' and '殊可怖也' means 'it is truly terrifying.'
  • 凝台顾余笑曰：非轿夫不能作此言 - Ningtai looked at me and laughed, saying, 'Only a sedan chair bearer could say such a thing.' '凝台顾余' means 'Ningtai looked at me,' '笑曰' means 'laughed and said,' and '非轿夫不能作此言' means 'only a sedan chair bearer could say such a thing.'

  • 某甲恒五鼓出担粪 - Reference to a daily routine; 'Majia always goes out to carry manure at the fifth drum (dawn).'

  • 社公何灵哉 - Rhetorical question expressing doubt; 'How powerful is the village god?'
