Idiomatic Expressions

  • 孔氏之門 - Refers to the Confucian school or tradition.
  • 本無此五十五點之圖矣 - Means 'originally did not have this diagram of fifty-five points,' indicating that the diagram is not part of the original Confucian tradition.
  • 陳抟何自而得之 - A rhetorical question meaning 'how did Chen Tuan obtain it,' implying that the origin of the diagram is unclear or mysterious.
  • 洛書既謂之書，當有文字 - Means 'since the Luo Shu is called a book, it should have writing,' suggesting that the Luo Shu should contain written characters.
  • 四十五圈，與河圖相同 - Refers to the Luo Shu having forty-five circles, similar to the He Tu (River Map), another classical diagram.
  • 宜稱洛圖，不得稱書 - Means 'it should be called the Luo Tu (Luo Diagram) rather than the Luo Shu (Luo Book),' indicating a distinction between the two terms.
  • 系詞又何以別之曰書乎 - A rhetorical question meaning 'how does the *Xici* (Commentary on the Appended Phrases) distinguish it as a book,' suggesting that the *Xici* should clarify the term.
  • 劉向劉歆班固，並稱洛書有文 - Means 'Liu Xiang, Liu Xin, and Ban Gu all called the Luo Shu to have writing,' indicating that these scholars believed the Luo Shu contained written characters.
  • 孔穎達尚書正義並詳載其字數 - Means 'Kong Yingda's *Shangshu Zhengyi* (Commentary on the Book of Documents) meticulously records the number of characters,' indicating that Kong Yingda's work provides detailed information about the Luo Shu.
  • 洪範初一曰五行一章 - Refers to the first chapter of the *Hongfan* (Great Pattern) section of the *Shangshu* (Book of Documents), which discusses the Five Elements.
  • 大劉及顧氏以為龜背先有 - Means 'the elder Liu and Gu believed that the turtle shell already had these characters,' suggesting that these scholars thought the characters were pre-existing.
  • 小劉以為敬用等皆禹所敘第 - Means 'the younger Liu believed that '敬用' and others were all arranged by Yu,' suggesting that this scholar thought Yu arranged the characters.
  • 其龜文惟有二十字云云 - Means 'the turtle shell only had twenty characters,' indicating that this scholar believed the turtle shell contained only twenty characters.
  • 未可執盧辨晚出之說 - Means 'it is not advisable to cling to Lu Bian's later theory,' indicating that Lu Bian's theory should not be accepted.
  • 明堂九室法龜文，始見北齊盧辨大戴禮注 - Means 'the theory that the nine rooms of the Mingtang (Hall of Brightness) follow the pattern of the turtle shell first appeared in Lu Bian's commentary on the *Dai Liji* (Great Records of Rites) during the Northern Qi dynasty,' indicating the origin of this theory.
  • 朱子以鄭康成說，偶誤記也 - Means 'Zhu Xi mistakenly recorded Vajra's (Zheng Xuan's) theory,' indicating that Zhu Xi made an error in his recording.
  • 遂以太乙九宮真為神禹所受也 - Means 'thus, the Taiyi Nine Palaces Diagram is truly what the divine Yu received,' indicating that the Taiyi Nine Palaces Diagram is attributed to Yu.
  • 今術家所用洛書，乃太乙行九宮法，出於易緯乾鑿度 - Means 'the Luo Shu used by modern practitioners is the Taiyi Nine Palaces method, derived from the *Yi Lü* (Book of Changes Apocrypha) *Gan Zao Du* (Tilling the Earth),' indicating the origin of the modern Luo Shu.
  • 即漢書藝文志所謂太乙家，當明原不稱為洛書也 - Means 'this is what the *Han Shu* (Book of Han) *Yi Wen Zhi* (Catalogue of Arts and Literature) calls the Taiyi school, and it should be clearly understood that it is not called the Luo Shu,' indicating that the Taiyi school should not be confused with the Luo Shu.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 陳抟 - A famous Taoist scholar and alchemist from the Tang and Song dynasties.
  • 洛書 - The Luo Shu (Luo Book), a classical diagram associated with the Luo River.
  • 河圖 - The He Tu (River Map), another classical diagram associated with the Yellow River.
  • 孔穎達 - A Tang dynasty scholar who wrote the *Shangshu Zhengyi* (Commentary on the Book of Documents).
  • 洪範 - The *Hongfan* (Great Pattern) section of the *Shangshu* (Book of Documents), which discusses the Five Elements.
  • 盧辨 - A scholar from the Northern Qi dynasty who wrote commentaries on classical texts.
  • 朱子 - Zhu Xi, a prominent Neo-Confucian scholar from the Song dynasty.
  • 鄭康成 - Zheng Xuan, a Han dynasty scholar known for his commentaries on classical texts.
  • 太乙九宮 - The Taiyi Nine Palaces Diagram, a method used in divination and feng shui.
  • 易緯乾鑿度 - The *Yi Lü* (Book of Changes Apocrypha) *Gan Zao Du* (Tilling the Earth), a text that discusses the origins of the *Yi Jing* (Book of Changes).

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence introduces the Confucian tradition and the mysterious origin of the diagram, with a rhetorical question about Chen Tuan's source.
  • This sentence argues that the Luo Shu should have writing and describes its similarity to the He Tu.
  • This sentence distinguishes between the terms 'Luo Tu' and 'Luo Shu' and questions the *Xici* (Commentary on the Appended Phrases) for not clarifying this distinction.
  • This sentence lists scholars who believed the Luo Shu had writing and mentions Kong Yingda's detailed record.
  • This sentence discusses the *Hongfan* (Great Pattern) and the detailed explanation of the characters in the Luo Shu.
  • This sentence explains that twenty-seven characters were added by Yu.
  • This sentence describes the differing opinions of scholars on the origin of the characters.
  • This sentence concludes that despite differing accounts, the Luo Shu did not have a false diagram of black and white dots.
  • This sentence suggests that the stone patterns are a reliable source of the characters.
  • This sentence advises against accepting Lu Bian's later theory.
  • This sentence indicates the origin of the theory that the nine rooms of the Mingtang (Hall of Brightness) follow the pattern of the turtle shell.
  • This sentence indicates that Zhu Xi made an error in his recording of Zheng Xuan's theory.
  • This sentence attributes the Taiyi Nine Palaces Diagram to Yu.
  • This sentence indicates the origin of the modern Luo Shu.
  • This sentence clarifies that the Taiyi school should not be confused with the Luo Shu.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 道家言祈禳，佛家言忏悔，儒家则言修德以胜妖 - A comparative statement contrasting the methods of Daoism, Buddhism, and Confucianism in dealing with evil or misfortune.
  • 二氏治其末，儒者治其本也 - A judgment that Daoism and Buddhism address the symptoms while Confucianism addresses the root cause.
  • 石言于晋，左传之义明矣 - A reference to a historical event from the *Zuo Zhuan* illustrating the principle of divine warning.
  • 祸已成欤，杀羊何益 - A rhetorical question challenging the effectiveness of killing the sheep after the harm has already occurred.
  • 祸未成而鬼神以是警余也，修德而已 - A statement emphasizing the importance of moral cultivation as a response to divine warning.
  • 岂在杀羊 - A rhetorical question dismissing the idea that killing the sheep is the solution.
  • 自是一言一动，如对圣贤 - A statement describing the change in behavior after realizing the necessity of moral cultivation.
  • 骈拇枝指，亦异于众，可曰妖乎哉 - A rhetorical question challenging the classification of physical anomalies as 'monsters' or 'demons.'
  • 一人右手掌大如箕，指大如椎，而左手则如常 - A description of a person with a large right hand and a normal left hand.
  • 使谈谶纬者见之，必曰此豕祸，此牛祸，此人疴也，是将兆某患 - A hypothetical scenario where superstitious people would interpret the anomalies as omens of disaster.
  • 然余所见诸异，讫毫无征验也 - A statement concluding that the anomalies had no actual consequences.
  • 故余于汉儒之学最不信春秋阴阳、洪范五行，传于宋儒之学最不信河图洛书、皇级经世 - A statement of disbelief in certain traditional scholarly beliefs.

  • 石言于晋 - A historical event from the *Zuo Zhuan* where a stone spoke in the state of Jin, warning of impending disaster.
  • 左传 - The *Zuo Zhuan*, a classical Chinese text that provides commentary on the *Spring and Autumn Annals*.
  • 顺治乙酉 - A reference to the year 1655 during the reign of the Shunzhi Emperor.
  • 雍正丁未 - A reference to the year 1727 during the reign of the Yongzheng Emperor.

  • 河图洛书 - A pair of texts that describe the origins of the *Book of Changes* and the *Book of Documents*.
  • 皇级经世 - A text that describes the principles of governance and the universe.

  • 半载后病瘵，夜嗽不能寝，散步林下，闻有笑语声，偶往寻视 - Describes the narrator's illness with consumption, his inability to sleep due to coughing, and his subsequent actions of taking a walk in the forest and investigating the laughter and talking.
  • 见屋数楹有人，拥我妇坐石看月，不胜恚忿，力疾欲与角 - Describes the scene where the narrator saw several houses with people, his wife surrounded and sitting on a stone watching the moon, and his reaction of anger and wanting to fight.
  • 其人亦怒曰：鼠辈乃敢瞰我妇，亦奋起相搏 - Describes the other person's reaction, calling the narrator a scoundrel for daring to look at his wife, and the ensuing fight.
  • 幸其亦病惫，相牵并仆，妇安坐石上，嬉笑曰：尔辈勿斗，吾明告尔，吾实往来于两家，皆托云上直，使尔辈休息五日，蓄精以供采补耳 - Describes the outcome of the fight, where both were weak and fell together, and the wife's reaction of calmly sitting on the stone and laughing, revealing her true intentions of moving between the two families to let them rest and store energy for replenishing.

  • 人对狼口，宜其不蕃也 - The phrase suggests that being opposite a place named 'Lang'er Kou' (狼儿口) is not favorable for prosperity.

  • 直北五里，曰木沽口 - Five miles directly north is a place called 'Muguku Kou' (木沽口).
  • 沽字土音在果戈之间 - The local pronunciation of 'gu' (沽) lies between the sounds of 'guo' (果) and 'ge' (戈).
  • 自改门后，人字洼渐富腴 - After changing the direction of the gate, the 'Renshi Wa' (人字洼) area gradually became prosperous.
  • 而木沽口渐雕敝矣 - Meanwhile, the 'Muguku Kou' (木沽口) area gradually became dilapidated.

  • 抑孤虚之说，竟真有之 - Or is it true that the theory of 'guxu' (孤虚), a concept in traditional Chinese geomancy, holds any truth?

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 披衣出视 - To put on clothes and go out to look, indicating a quick response to a disturbance.
  • 微月之下 - Under the dim moonlight, setting the scene at night.
  • 审谛 - To examine carefully, suggesting a closer look at the unusual sight.
  • 畜牛昂首而蹲 - Describes the cow standing on its hind legs with its head raised, which is an unusual posture.
  • 不知其何以上也 - Indicates confusion about how the cow managed to stand on its hind legs.
  • 喧传 - To spread by word of mouth, indicating the rapid dissemination of news.
  • 男妇皆来看异事 - Men and women all came to see the strange event, showing the community's curiosity.
  • 忽一家火发 - Suddenly, a house caught fire, indicating the sudden onset of the disaster.
  • 焰猛风狂 - Describes the intense flames and strong winds, emphasizing the severity of the fire.
  • 合村几尽为焦土 - The entire village was almost completely reduced to ashes, indicating the extent of the destruction.
  • 牛祸兆回禄也 - The cow's appearance was a harbinger of fire (回禄), linking the unusual event to the subsequent disaster.
  • 时方纳稼 - It was the time of harvest, indicating the season.
  • 豆秸谷草，堆秫篱茅屋间 - Describes the storage of harvested crops, emphasizing the flammable materials present.
  • 袤延相接 - Stretching out and touching each other, indicating the close proximity of the houses.
  • 农家作苦 - Farmers work hard, highlighting the labor-intensive nature of farming.
  • 家家夜半皆酣眠 - Every household was sleeping soundly at midnight, indicating the villagers' state before the disaster.
  • 突尔遭焚 - Suddenly, they were burned, indicating the unexpected nature of the fire.
  • 天心仁爱 - The benevolent heart of heaven, suggesting divine intervention.
  • 以此牛惊使梦醒也 - The cow's appearance caused the villagers to wake up from their sleep, linking the unusual event to the villagers' survival.
  • 何反以为妖哉 - Why was it considered a demon instead? expressing skepticism about the villagers' interpretation.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 田香谷 - The name of the person reporting the story, likely a local figure.
  • 景河镇 - The name of a town, providing the geographical setting.
  • 小村 - A small village, indicating the rural setting.
  • 邹某 - The name of the individual who first noticed the unusual event.
  • 回禄 - A term for fire, often associated with divine punishment or omens.
  • 时方纳稼 - Refers to the harvest season, a crucial time for farmers.
  • 豆秸谷草 - Harvested crops such as beans, straw, and grain, which were commonly stored in rural areas.
  • 堆秫篱茅屋间 - Describes the storage of these crops, often in simple, flammable structures.
  • 农家作苦 - Highlights the hard work of farmers, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
  • 天心仁爱 - Refers to the benevolence of heaven, a concept often discussed in classical Chinese philosophy and literature.

Proper Nouns

  • 田香谷 - The name of the person reporting the story.
  • 景河镇 - The name of the town.
  • 邹某 - The name of the individual who first noticed the unusual event.
  • 姚安公 - The name of the person providing the commentary.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 同郡某孝廉 - Refers to a certain candidate for the imperial examination (孝廉) from the same prefecture (同郡).
  • 落拓不羁 - Describes a person who is unrestrained and free-spirited.
  • 多来往青楼中 - Indicates frequent visits to brothels or places of entertainment.
  • 漠然也 - Means 'indifferent' or 'unconcerned.'
  • 独赏之 - Means 'only appreciated him.'
  • 此君岂长贫贱者哉 - A rhetorical question meaning 'Is this man destined to remain poor and lowly?'
  • 狎饮 - Refers to intimate or casual drinking.
  • 夜合资供其读书 - Means 'at night, they pooled money to support his studies.'
  • 应试 - Refers to taking an examination.
  • 捐金治装 - Means 'donated money to prepare his outfit.'
  • 谋薪米 - Means 'arranged for his daily necessities.'
  • 握臂与盟 - Means 'swore an oath by clasping arms.'
  • 纳汝 - Means 'take you as a wife.'
  • 重君者 - Means 'value you highly.'
  • 怪姊妹惟识富家儿 - Means 'it's strange that the sisters only recognize wealthy men.'
  • 脂粉绮罗中 - Refers to the world of beauty and luxury.
  • 巨眼人 - Means 'a person with discerning eyes.'
  • 白头之约 - Refers to a lifelong commitment.
  • 幽闭闺阁 - Means 'be confined in the inner chambers.'
  • 坐囹圄 - Means 'be imprisoned.'
  • 始相欢合，终致仳离 - Means 'start with mutual affection but end in separation.'
  • 作长相思 - Means 'maintain a long-lasting longing.'
  • 为县令 - Means 'became a county magistrate.'
  • 招之不赴 - Means 'invited but did not respond.'
  • 车马日稀 - Means 'visits became less frequent.'
  • 终未尝一至其署 - Means 'never once visited his office.'
  • 奇女子 - Means 'a remarkable woman.'
  • 鸟尽弓藏 - A metaphor for betrayal after achieving success, often used in the context of historical figures like Han Xin.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 孝廉 - A title given to candidates for the imperial examination, indicating their moral integrity and scholarly ability.
  • 青楼 - Refers to brothels or places of entertainment, often associated with courtesans.
  • 县令 - The title of a county magistrate, a local government official.
  • 鸟尽弓藏 - A metaphor for betrayal after achieving success, often used in the context of historical figures like Han Xin, who was betrayed by Liu Bang after contributing to the establishment of the Han dynasty.

Proper Nouns

  • 孝廉 - A title for candidates for the imperial examination.
  • 县令 - The title of a county magistrate.
  • 韩淮阴 - A historical figure, Han Xin, a general under Liu Bang during the establishment of the Han dynasty.
  • 鸟尽弓藏 - A metaphor for betrayal after achieving success.
